advantages: Provides a principled way to reverse conditioning—from P(evidence|hypothesis) to P(hypothesis|evidence)—which is what decision-makers actually need.
Combines multiple information sources (prior belief + new data) in one transparent formula instead of ad hoc rules.
Works for any finite partition of hypotheses; extendable to continuous parameters via PDFs and integrals.
Makes base rates explicit: forces you to account for how rare or common A is before interpreting striking evidence B.
Foundation for Naive Bayes, Bayesian A/B tests, and probabilistic reasoning in safety-critical ML pipelines.
disadvantages: Requires a correct or defensible prior P(A); a wrong prior can dominate the posterior when data is scarce.
Demands an accurate denominator P(B); misspecifying the law of total probability yields systematically wrong posteriors.
Likelihood P(B|A) can be hard to model in high dimensions without simplifying assumptions (e.g. Naive Bayes independence).
Exact Bayesian updating is computationally expensive for complex models; practitioners often approximate or switch to point estimates (MLE).
Easy to misinterpret when events are not mutually exclusive or when the partition assumption is violated.
